title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant file read via NativeEnvironmentRepository
description: This vulnerability affects the native backend of Spring Cloud Config Server and results from insufficient validation of user-controlled request parameters. An unauthenticated attacker can supply wildcard characters that are interpreted by Spring Boot as directory expansion patterns. This may cause the server to load and return configuration files from multiple applications or tenants outside the intended scope. Exploitation could expose sensitive information such as database credentials, API tokens, OAuth secrets, cloud credentials, and signing keys.
impact: An unauthenticated attacker can exploit this issue to disclose configuration files across multiple applications or tenants, potentially exposing sensitive credentials, secrets, and tokens.

title: Unauthenticated SSRF in `nuxt-og-image` via `fonts[].path` URL parameter
description: nuxt-og-image fails to properly validate the `fonts[].path` URL parameter, allowing unauthenticated attackers to perform SSRF attacks. This can be exploited to access internal services and potentially exfiltrate sensitive information.
impact: An unauthenticated attacker can make the server perform arbitrary HTTP requests, potentially accessing internal services and sensitive data.

title: Arbitrary File Read via World-Writable /tmp Allowed-Root + Check-Then-Use (TOCTOU) Sandbox Bypass
description: NLTK's default file I/O sandbox can be bypassed because it trusts the system temporary directory and performs path validation separately from the actual file access operation. An attacker can exploit a race condition involving symlinks in /tmp to make the sandbox approve a harmless file while ultimately opening arbitrary files accessible to the process.
impact: An attacker can bypass NLTK's sandbox to read sensitive files and, through a separate arbitrary file write flaw, potentially achieve remote code execution.

title: Path traversal in camel-azure-storage-blob / camel-azure-storage-datalake downloadToFile
description: The components construct local file paths directly from attacker-controlled blob or file names without normalizing the path or verifying that the resulting file remains inside the intended download directory. By uploading objects containing ../ sequences, an attacker with access to the monitored Azure container or file system can cause files to be written outside the configured directory. The flaw affects both Azure components because they lack the directory-jail protections already implemented in other Camel file-handling modules.
impact: An attacker can overwrite arbitrary files on the host system as the Camel process user, potentially leading to remote code execution and data loss.

title: Arbitrary File Read and SSRF in Spring Cloud Stream via Message-Driven
description: Spring Cloud Function’s JsonMessageConverter allows attackers to control the class used for JSON deserialization when a Spring Cloud Stream consumer accepts messages as Object. By supplying a malicious contentType header, an untrusted message producer can force the application to instantiate arbitrary classes available on the classpath. Several Spring PropertyEditor implementations expose dangerous side effects during deserialization, enabling local file access and outbound network requests to attacker-controlled or internal systems.
impact: An attacker can trigger arbitrary class instantiation to read sensitive files and perform blind SSRF, potentially leading to credential theft and further compromise.

title: Unconditional RCE via JoltTransform.customValidate -> URLClassLoader
description: Apache NiFi's parameter validation API allows users with only READ permissions to trigger component validation using attacker-controlled parameter values. When a JoltTransform processor externalizes its custom transformation settings through parameters, an attacker can supply a malicious JAR URL and class name during a validation request.
impact: A user with only READ access to a parameter context and a bound JoltTransform processor can achieve remote code execution on the NiFi host.

title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting in Aperture Admin Portal Tenant Display Name
description: The tenant 'display name' field is rendered without output encoding in the platform super-admin tenant roster. A low-privileged tenant operator stores a script payload that executes in a super-admin's browser the moment they open the tenant list. LoSec sample advisory — fictional product for illustration.
impact: Privilege escalation from tenant operator to platform super-admin via session-riding actions such as minting an admin API key. The scope change carries execution from the tenant context into the parent admin context.

title: Use of Hard-Coded Cryptographic Key in Helios Field SDK Offline Cache
description: The SDK encrypts its offline credential cache with a static AES key compiled into the shipped binary and identical across every install. The key is recoverable in seconds (a plain 'strings' pass over the bundled native library), after which any exfiltrated cache blob — from a device backup or seized hardware — can be decrypted. LoSec sample advisory — fictional product for illustration.
impact: Recovery of long-lived field-operator API tokens from device backups or lost/seized devices. The AC:H rating reflects the need to first obtain a cache blob; once obtained, decryption is deterministic. Coordinated disclosure ongoing.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference Exposes Cross-Tenant Models in Cortex Model Registry
description: GET /api/v1/models/{id}/download authorizes on authentication only, never verifying tenant ownership of the requested object. Model IDs are sequential integers, so any authenticated user can enumerate and download other tenants' proprietary model weights. LoSec sample advisory — fictional product for illustration.
impact: Wholesale theft of other tenants' model intellectual property and any training data embedded in the weights. Publicly disclosed after the vendor exceeded the agreed 90-day window without a remediation.

title: Sensitive Enrollment Token Disclosure in Meshwork Edge Agent Debug Log
description: With log level set to debug, the agent writes the full enrollment bearer token in cleartext to /var/log/meshwork/agent.log, which ships world-readable (mode 0644). Any local low-privileged user can read the token from the log. LoSec sample advisory — fictional product for illustration.
impact: A local user harvests the edge enrollment token and can re-enroll or impersonate the device to the orchestrator. Severity is constrained by the requirement for local access and a non-default debug log level.
